content.problemStatement:
Kekocity's population consist of **N** gnomes numbered with unique ids from **1** to **N**. As they are very joyful gnomes, they usually send jokes to their friends right after they get any (even if they knew it before) via their social network named as Mybeard. Mybeard became popular in the city because of message auto-deletion. It takes exactly one minute to read and resend joke to mates.


Mayor of Kekocity, Mr. Shaikhinidin, is interested in understanding how the jokes are spread. He gives you database of Mybeard social network, and wants you to answer some queries on it.


You will be given a list of friends for every gnome and **M** queries of the following type: Who will receive a message with joke after exactly **K** minutes, if the creator of joke was gnome with id **x**?
content.inputFormat: The first line contains a single integer N denoting the number of gnomes. The next N lines contain the matrix g[N][N]. Each of the i-th line, will contain N space separated integers - j-th of those will denote g[i][j]. If gnome j is friend of gnome i, then g[i][j] is 1. Otherwise it will be zero. The next line contains a single integer M denoting the number of queries. The next M lines contain two integers k and x described above.
content.outputFormat: For each query, output two lines. In the first line, output how many gnomes will know the joke after k minutes. In the second line, print these ids (numbering) of these gnomes in increasing order. If no one will know the joke after K minutes, then print -1 in this line.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The first query asks who will receive the message after 3 minutes if the creator of the joke was gnome 1. The answer is gnomes 1 and 4. The second query asks who will receive the message after 10000 minutes if the creator of the joke was gnome 1. The answer is gnomes 2 and 4. The third query asks who will receive the message after 0 minutes if the creator of the joke was gnome 5. The answer is gnome 5. The fourth query asks who will receive the message after 1 minute if the creator of the joke was gnome 5. The answer is no one.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use a Breadth-First Search (BFS) algorithm. We start by initializing a queue with the source gnome and a set to keep track of visited gnomes. Then, we perform BFS for k minutes, adding all unvisited friends of the current gnome to the queue and marking them as visited. Finally, we return the number of gnomes that will know the joke after k minutes and their ids.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N + M * (N + k)) — The time complexity is dominated by the BFS traversal for each query.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) — The space complexity is dominated by the queue and the set of visited gnomes.
